fixes.napkin_math: We calculate the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) as `TCO = Annual Compute Cost + Annual Engineering Cost + Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)`. **Strategy A: Centralized Cloud Training.** Compute: fine-tune on 4× A100s for 2 weeks/quarter = 4 × $2.20/hr × 336hr × 4 = $11,827/year. Engineering: 1 ML engineer at $200K/year (half-time) = $100K. ALE: probability of a HIPAA breach × cost. With PHI centralized in one cloud tenant, assume 5% annual breach probability × $10M average cost (fines + lawsuits + reputation) = $500K ALE. **Strategy A TCO ≈ $612K/year.** **Strategy B: Federated Learning.** Compute: 10 hospital edge nodes at $500/month each + 1 aggregation server at $1,000/month = $72K/year. Engineering: 2 ML engineers (FL is harder) = $400K. ALE: PHI never leaves hospitals, breach probability drops to 0.5% × $2M (smaller blast radius) = $10K ALE. **Strategy B TCO ≈ $482K/year.** The FL approach saves ~$130K/year, driven almost entirely by the 50× reduction in ALE. The engineering cost is higher, but the risk-adjusted TCO is decisively lower.

**Strategy A: Centralized Training (3-year TCO).** Data acquisition: 10 hospitals × $50K licensing fee = $500K one-time. Data transfer: 10 hospitals × 10 TB × $0.09/GB egress = $9,000. Central storage: 100 TB × $0.023/GB/month × 36 months = $82,800. Compute: 8× A100 cluster at $100/hr, training 4 weeks/year × 3 years = $100 × 672hr × 3 = $201,600. Engineering: 1 ML engineer (half-time) × $200K × 3 years = $300,000. **Strategy A 3-year TCO ≈ $1.09M.** **Strategy B: Federated Learning (3-year TCO).** Data acquisition fee: $0 (data stays on-premise). Data transfer: only gradient updates, negligible. On-prem GPU nodes: 10 × $15K amortized over 3 years = $150K. Aggregation server: $2,000/month × 36 = $72K. Engineering: 2 ML engineers (FL expertise) × $200K × 3 = $1.2M. **Strategy B 3-year TCO ≈ $1.42M.** Strategy B costs ~$330K more in direct costs. However, the non-quantifiable risk of centralizing 100 TB of medical imaging data (HIPAA fines up to $1.8M per violation category, plus reputational damage) makes Strategy B the safer long-term bet for a health-tech startup. A single breach could exceed the entire 3-year cost difference.

Let's analyze the Arithmetic Intensity (AI) using the Roofline Model. AI = FLOPs / Bytes transferred. **H100 ridge point:** 989 TFLOPS / 3.35 TB/s = **295 FLOPs/byte**. Any operation below this AI is memory-bound. **Dense 70B model (batch=1 decode):** Each token loads all 70B params (140 GB in FP16) and performs 2 × 70B = 140 GFLOPs. AI = 140 GFLOPs / 140 GB = **1 FLOP/byte** — deeply memory-bound. Achievable throughput limited by bandwidth: 3.35 TB/s / 140 GB = **24 tokens/s**. **50% unstructured pruning:** FLOPs halved to 70 GFLOPs, but sparse weights still require loading indices + values. Effective memory traffic ≈ 100 GB (70 GB values + 30 GB index overhead with CSR format). AI = 70 / 100 = **0.7 FLOPs/byte** — even MORE memory-bound. Worse, non-coalesced memory access patterns reduce effective bandwidth to ~1.5 TB/s. Actual throughput: 1.5 TB/s / 100 GB = **15 tokens/s** — a 37% slowdown despite 50% fewer FLOPs. **Distilled 35B dense model:** Loads 70 GB, performs 70 GFLOPs. AI = 70 / 70 = **1 FLOP/byte** — same ratio, but fully coalesced access uses the full 3.35 TB/s. Throughput: 3.35 TB/s / 70 GB = **48 tokens/s** — a 2× speedup with perfect hardware utilization.

Let's analyze the memory access of the attention matrix during prefill for Llama-70B on H100. **Standard attention prefill at 32K tokens:** For each attention head, the QK^T matrix is [32000 × 32000] in FP16 = 2 GB per head. With 64 heads across 80 layers, total attention matrix memory = 64 × 80 × 2 GB = **10.24 TB** of intermediate memory traffic. This must be written to HBM and read back for softmax — 2 round trips = **20.48 TB** of HBM traffic. At 3.35 TB/s bandwidth: 20.48 / 3.35 = **6.1 seconds** just for attention memory traffic. **With FlashAttention:** Attention is computed in tiles that stay in SRAM (256 KB per SM). The Q, K, V matrices themselves are only 2 × 80 × 8 × 128 × 32000 × 2 bytes ≈ 10 GB (using GQA with 8 KV heads). Memory traffic drops from ~20 TB to ~10 GB — a **2000× reduction**. Prefill time drops to <1 second, dominated by the 2 × 70B × 32000 = 4,480 TFLOPs of compute at 989 TFLOPS = **4.5 seconds** (compute-bound, not memory-bound). **Speculative decoding impact on TTFT:** Zero. Speculative decoding accelerates the decode phase (token-by-token generation) by batching verification. It does not reduce the prefill phase, which is the TTFT bottleneck for RAG workloads with long prompts.

Let's compare the compute for generating 3 accepted tokens. Assume the draft model (7B) proposes a block of k=4 tokens and the first 3 are accepted. **Without speculative decoding (autoregressive 70B):** 3 sequential forward passes of the 70B model. FLOPs = 3 × 2 × 70B = **420 GFLOPs**. Wall time: 3 sequential memory-bound steps. At batch=1 decode, each step loads 140 GB of weights. Time = 3 × (140 GB / 3.35 TB/s) = 3 × 41.8ms = **125ms**. **With speculative decoding:** Draft phase: 4 sequential forward passes of 7B model. FLOPs = 4 × 2 × 7B = 56 GFLOPs. Time = 4 × (14 GB / 3.35 TB/s) = 4 × 4.2ms = 16.7ms. Verification phase: 1 forward pass of 70B model on 4 tokens (batched). FLOPs = 2 × 70B × 4 = 560 GFLOPs. Time ≈ 42ms (parallel verification, same weight load as one step). Total time: 16.7 + 42 = **58.7ms** for 3 tokens — a **2.1× speedup**. **But total FLOPs:** 56 + 560 = **616 GFLOPs** vs 420 GFLOPs — a **47% increase** in total compute. The cost increase comes from: (1) the draft model's wasted FLOPs (the rejected 4th token), and (2) the verification pass processes all 4 tokens even though only 3 are accepted. You pay for more total GPU-seconds (draft + verifier running), but wall-clock latency drops because the sequential bottleneck is broken.

Let's model the H100's 80 GB HBM. **Setup:** 34B model weights in FP16 = 68 GB. Free HBM for KV cache: 80 − 68 = **12 GB** (not 40 GB — the scenario's nvidia-smi reading of 40 GB likely counts memory reserved but not yet allocated by the framework). **Fragmentation example with contiguous allocation:** Suppose 4 requests arrive: A (3 GB), B (2 GB), C (4 GB), D (3 GB) = 12 GB total, filling memory. Requests B and D complete, freeing 2 + 3 = 5 GB. Free memory: **5 GB in two non-contiguous holes** (2 GB and 3 GB). A new request E needs 4 GB. Despite 5 GB free, no single contiguous block fits 4 GB → **OOM**. This is external fragmentation. **With PagedAttention:** KV cache is divided into fixed-size pages (e.g., 256 KB blocks, enough for 16 tokens of KV state). Request E's 4 GB = 16,384 pages. These pages can be scattered anywhere in physical HBM — no contiguous requirement. The 2 GB + 3 GB holes provide 20,480 pages — more than enough. Fragmentation waste drops from the size of the largest gap to **at most one page per request** (internal fragmentation only). With 256 KB pages and 100 concurrent requests: worst-case waste = 100 × 256 KB = **25 MB** — negligible.

Let's quantify the 'wait time' disaster. **Assumptions:** H100 cluster serves both tiers from a single FIFO queue. Standard document analysis jobs: average 30-second processing time, arriving at 10 jobs/min during the launch surge. Premium dialogue requests: average 200ms processing time, requiring P99 TTFT < 200ms. **During the product launch surge:** Queue depth builds: 10 Standard jobs/min × 30s each = 5 GPU-minutes of work entering per minute. If cluster capacity is 8 GPUs, steady-state queue wait for any new request = queue_depth × avg_service_time / num_GPUs. With 50 Standard jobs queued: wait time = 50 × 30s / 8 = **187 seconds** for a Premium request arriving at the back of the FIFO. This is **935× the 200ms SLA**. **Adding 8 more GPUs (playbook action):** Wait time = 50 × 30s / 16 = **93 seconds** — still 465× the SLA. The problem is not capacity; it is ordering. Even with infinite GPUs, if the scheduler processes the queue in FIFO order and Standard jobs saturate the queue, Premium requests still wait behind them. **With priority queuing:** Premium requests jump to the front. Wait time = (0 Standard jobs ahead) × 30s + (2 Premium jobs ahead) × 0.2s / 8 GPUs = **50ms** — well within the 200ms SLA, with zero additional hardware.

Let's quantify this using the Roofline Model. **H100 specs:** 989 TFLOPS FP16, 3.35 TB/s HBM bandwidth. Ridge point = 989,000 / 3,350 = **295 FLOPs/byte**. **B200 specs (approximate):** ~4,500 TFLOPS FP16 (~4.5× H100), ~8 TB/s bandwidth (~2.4× H100). Ridge point = 4,500,000 / 8,000 = **562 FLOPs/byte**. **Large LLM (e.g., 70B, batch=64 prefill):** Arithmetic intensity ≈ 64 × 2 FLOPs / 2 bytes = **64 FLOPs/byte** (reusing weights across batch). On H100: 64 < 295, so memory-bound. Throughput = 3.35 TB/s × 64 = 214 TFLOPS. On B200: 64 < 562, still memory-bound. Throughput = 8 TB/s × 64 = 512 TFLOPS. **Speedup: 512/214 = 2.4×** — matching the bandwidth improvement, not the compute improvement. Good ROI. **MobileNet (batch=1 inference):** ~300M FLOPs, ~13 MB weights. AI = 300M / 13M = **23 FLOPs/byte**. On H100: memory-bound. Throughput = 3.35 TB/s × 23 = 77 TFLOPS. On B200: still memory-bound. Throughput = 8 TB/s × 23 = 184 TFLOPS. **Speedup: 184/77 = 2.4×** — but at 3× the price. Cost-efficiency is **0.8× the H100** — you pay 3× more for 2.4× speedup. The B200 only wins on price-performance for workloads above its ridge point (562 FLOPs/byte), e.g., large-batch training.

First, calculate the theoretical ridge points. **H100:** Peak FP16 = 989 TFLOPS. Memory bandwidth = 3.35 TB/s. Ridge point = 989,000 GFLOPs / 3,350 GB/s = **295 FLOPs/byte**. **Prometheus-1:** Peak FP16 = 5 × 989 = 4,945 TFLOPS. Memory bandwidth = 2 × 3.35 = 6.7 TB/s. Ridge point = 4,945,000 / 6,700 = **738 FLOPs/byte**. **Analysis:** Any workload with arithmetic intensity below 738 FLOPs/byte is memory-bound on the Prometheus-1 and cannot exploit its 5× compute advantage. Between 295 and 738 FLOPs/byte: the workload is compute-bound on H100 (benefiting from peak FLOPs) but memory-bound on Prometheus-1 (limited by bandwidth). The Prometheus-1 only achieves its advertised 5× speedup for workloads ABOVE 738 FLOPs/byte. **Practical workloads:** LLM decode (batch=1): AI ≈ 1 FLOP/byte → memory-bound on both. Prometheus-1 speedup = 6.7/3.35 = **2× at 3× cost — net loss**. LLM training (large batch): AI ≈ 200–500 FLOPs/byte → compute-bound on H100, memory-bound on Prometheus-1. Speedup = 6.7/3.35 × AI / AI = **2× — still a loss**. Only dense matmuls at batch >1024: AI > 1000 FLOPs/byte → **5× speedup at 3× cost = 1.67× better cost-efficiency**. **Recommendation:** Purchase only if >60% of workload hours are large-batch training or dense inference at high batch sizes.

Let's quantify the proposed mixed-precision solution. An 8×22B MoE model (Mixtral architecture) has: shared layers (embeddings, attention) ≈ ~5B params, plus 8 expert FFN blocks × 22B params each ≈ 176B. **Total parameters ≈ 181B** (not 141B). **Memory in FP16:** 181B × 2 bytes = **362 GB** — requires at least 5× H100 80GB GPUs. **After naive INT8 quantization:** 181B × 1 byte = **181 GB** — still requires 3× H100s, and quality collapsed due to gating network quantization. **Mixed-precision strategy:** (1) Gating network (~100M params): keep in FP16 = 0.2 GB. (2) Attention + embeddings (~5B params): FP16 = 10 GB. (3) Expert FFN weights (176B params): INT8 = 176 GB. **Total: ~186 GB** — fits on 3× H100s. **Cost comparison:** Original FP16 (5 GPUs): 5 × $3.50/hr = $17.50/hr. Mixed-precision (3 GPUs): 3 × $3.50/hr = $10.50/hr. **Savings: 40%** — meeting the VP's mandate while preserving routing quality. The critical insight: the gating network is <0.1% of total parameters but controls 100% of expert routing decisions. Keeping it in full precision preserves the routing distribution that the model learned during training.
fixes.realistic_solution: The core insight is that not all parameters are created equal, especially in an MoE architecture. The catastrophic failure is caused by quantizing the **gating network's logits**. This tiny part of the model determines which experts are routed to for each token. These logits have an extremely high dynamic range and are sensitive to tiny variations — they are not distributed like typical weights. Quantizing them to INT8 collapses their expressive power, leading to poor routing decisions (e.g., always picking the same few experts, or 'expert collapse'). The total model has ~181B parameters (not 141B): 8 expert blocks × 22B = 176B for experts, plus ~5B for shared attention and embeddings. In FP16 this requires ~362 GB — far too large for a single H100. The initial INT8 quantization reduced this to ~181 GB, fitting on 3 H100s, but destroyed routing quality. The fix is a mixed-precision strategy: keep the gating network and attention layers in FP16 while quantizing only the expert FFN weights to INT8.

fixes.napkin_math: 70B Llama model (80 layers, 8 KV heads via GQA, 128 head_dim), 2,000-token system prompt. **KV cache per token:** 2 (K+V) × 80 (layers) × 8 (kv_heads) × 128 (head_dim) × 2 bytes (FP16) = **327,680 bytes ≈ 320 KB/token**. **System prompt KV cache:** 2,000 tokens × 320 KB = **640 MB**. **Without caching at 1,000 req/min:** Each request recomputes and stores its own copy. Peak concurrent requests (assuming 2s avg generation): ~33 concurrent. Memory: 33 × 640 MB = **21.1 GB** duplicated KV cache across the cluster. Compute: 2,000-token prefill per request = 2 × 70B × 2,000 = 280 TFLOPs per request. At 1,000 req/min = 16.7 req/s: prefill load = 16.7 × 280 = **4,676 TFLOPS/s** — nearly 5× a single H100's capacity (989 TFLOPS), explaining the need for 4 GPUs. **With prefix caching:** Store one copy of the system prompt's KV cache (640 MB) shared across all requests. Memory savings: 21.1 GB → 640 MB = **20.5 GB freed** for more concurrent requests. Compute savings: skip 2,000-token prefill entirely. At 280 TFLOPs saved × 16.7 req/s = **4,676 TFLOPS/s saved** — equivalent to freeing ~4.7 H100s worth of prefill compute. This could allow serving the same traffic on 2 GPUs instead of 4, halving cost.

A slow Python BPE tokenizer might process 100,000 tokens per second. A 5,000-token prompt takes 50ms to tokenize on CPU. An H100 prefilling that same 5,000-token prompt for a 70B model requires 2 × 70B × 5,000 = 700 TFLOPs of compute. At ~50% utilization of 989 TFLOPS FP16: 700 / 495 ≈ **1,400ms** (~1.4 seconds). So the tokenizer's 50ms is only ~3.5% of the total prefill time — but it is a **serial CPU bottleneck** that blocks the GPU entirely. While the GPU sits idle during tokenization, it wastes $3.50/hr × 50ms = $0.000049 per request. At 1,000 req/min: 50ms × 1000 = 50 seconds of GPU idle time per minute — the GPU is stalled **83% of the time** if requests arrive back-to-back. Fix: use a Rust-based tokenizer (e.g., HuggingFace `tokenizers` library) that processes 1M+ tokens/s, reducing tokenization to <5ms, or run tokenization asynchronously on a separate CPU thread pool so the GPU is never blocked.
fixes.realistic_solution: You are hitting the **Tokenizer CPU Bottleneck**. A slow Python BPE tokenizer might process 100,000 tokens per second. A 5,000-token prompt takes 50ms to tokenize. However, the H100 prefilling that same prompt for a 70B model takes ~1.4 seconds (700 TFLOPs at ~50% GPU utilization), so the 50ms tokenizer overhead is small relative to prefill. The real issue is that tokenization is **synchronous and blocking**: the GPU sits completely idle (0% utilization) while the CPU tokenizes. In a high-throughput serving system, this serial bottleneck prevents request pipelining. The fix is to use a fast Rust-based tokenizer (HuggingFace `tokenizers`, ~1M+ tokens/s) and run tokenization asynchronously in a thread pool, overlapping CPU tokenization of the next request with GPU inference of the current one.

fixes.napkin_math: LLaVA-1.5 13B on A100-80GB (based on Llama-2 13B: 40 layers, 40 attention heads, 40 KV heads, 128 head_dim): | **Vision encoder (ViT-L/14):** 304M params. Forward pass on 4K image: ~50ms. | **Visual tokens from 4K image:** (3840/14) × (2160/14) ≈ **42,196 tokens**. | **LLM prefill for 42K tokens:** 2 × 13B × 42,196 = 1,097 TFLOPs. On A100 (312 TFLOPS at ~50% utilization): 1,097/156 = **7.0 seconds**. Plus KV-cache allocation for 42K tokens: 2 × 40 × 40 × 128 × 2 bytes × 42,196 = 34.5 GB per request — consuming nearly half the A100's 80 GB HBM. | **At 768×768 resolution:** (768/14)² ≈ **3,025 tokens**. Prefill: 2 × 13B × 3,025 = 78.7 TFLOPs / 156 = **504ms**. KV-cache: 2 × 40 × 40 × 128 × 2 × 3,025 = 2.47 GB. **14× faster TTFT, 14× less KV memory**. | **Trade-off:** Downsampling to 768×768 loses fine-grained detail (can't read small text in documents). Adaptive resolution (high-res crops only for regions of interest) balances quality and speed.

**Ring AllReduce formula:** The algorithm has two phases (scatter-reduce + all-gather), each requiring N−1 steps where N = number of GPUs. In each step, each GPU sends and receives a chunk of size `data_size / N`. Total data each GPU sends = `2 × (N−1)/N × data_size`. **Calculation:** N = 8 GPUs, data_size = 80 GB gradients. Each GPU's per-step chunk: 80 GB / 8 = 10 GB. Total steps: 2 × (8−1) = 14 steps. Per step, each GPU sends 10 GB over its NVLink connection. **Bandwidth:** The 900 GB/s is the *aggregate* bidirectional NVLink bandwidth per GPU. In a ring topology, each GPU uses one link to send to its neighbor. The effective unidirectional bandwidth per link ≈ 900 / 18 links × 2 (bidirectional) ≈ 50 GB/s per link per direction (NVLink 4.0 has 18 links at 25 GB/s each unidirectional = 450 GB/s unidirectional total). In the ring, each GPU sends on one set of links to one neighbor. Effective per-ring-link bandwidth ≈ **450 GB/s** (all links can be used to one neighbor in optimized implementations). **Time per phase:** (N−1)/N × data_size / bandwidth = 7/8 × 80 GB / 450 GB/s = **0.156 seconds**. **Total AllReduce time:** 2 × 0.156 = **0.31 seconds**. In practice, with overhead and less-than-perfect link utilization, expect ~0.4–0.5 seconds for 80 GB across 8 NVLink-connected H100s.

**Checkpoint size:** 175B params × 16 bytes (FP16 weights + FP32 master weights + FP32 Adam m + FP32 Adam v = 2+4+4+4 = 14 bytes, plus 2 bytes FP16 gradients = 16 bytes) = **2.8 TB**. **Write time:** Parallel write across 1,250 nodes to a distributed filesystem at ~10 GB/s aggregate = 2,800 / 10 = **~280 seconds** (~4.7 min) per checkpoint. **Step time estimate:** With 175B params, global batch size of ~1,536, and 8-way tensor parallelism across 10,000 GPUs: ~1.5 seconds per step is realistic. 1,000 steps ≈ **25 minutes** of training. Checkpointing overhead: 4.7 min every 25 min = **~16% overhead**. **Lost compute:** 1,000 steps × 1.5s = 1,500 seconds of training. 10,000 GPUs × 1,500s = **15,000,000 GPU-seconds** = **4,167 GPU-hours**. At $3.50/GPU-hr = **$14,583 of lost compute**. **Restart cost:** Job scheduler queue wait: ~5 min. NCCL initialization for 10,000 GPUs: ~10 min. Checkpoint load (2.8 TB from storage): ~5 min. Warmup/verification: ~5 min. Total restart: **~25 minutes** of 10,000 idle GPUs = 4,167 GPU-hours = **$14,583 of idle cost**. **Total per-failure cost:** $14,583 (lost compute) + $14,583 (restart idle) = **$29,166**. Over a 90-day run with MTBF of ~8 hours at this scale, expect ~270 failures. Total failure cost: 270 × $29,166 = **$7.9M** — a line item that must be budgeted.

BF16 format: 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits (bits 7–14), 7 mantissa bits (bits 0–6). A flip in bit 14 (the most significant exponent bit) changes the exponent by ±128. Since the stored exponent uses a bias of 127, flipping this bit changes the actual exponent by 128, multiplying or dividing the value by **2^128 ≈ 3.4 × 10^38**. A weight of 0.01 could become 0.01 × 2^128 ≈ 3.4 × 10^36 — effectively infinity in the context of neural network computations. Even a flip in a lower exponent bit (e.g., bit 10, changing exponent by 8) multiplies the value by 2^8 = 256×, turning 0.01 into 2.56. In a query projection matrix of shape [8192, 128], one corrupted weight at 2.56 (vs normal ~0.01) biases every token's query vector by +2.56 in one dimension. Attention logits shift by ~2.56 × typical_key_value ≈ **1–5 nats** — enough to redirect 50–90% of attention mass to wrong positions. **SDC rate at scale:** At 4,096 GPUs with 80 GB HBM each: total HBM = 327 TB. Google/Meta report SDC rates of ~1–2 per 1,000 GPU-days. Over 45 days: expected SDC events = 4,096 × 45 / 1,000 × 1.5 ≈ **276 events**. Most corrupt activations (transient, overwritten next step). But ~1% corrupt *weights* (persistent) = **~3 weight corruptions**. Detection: per-layer weight norm monitoring adds <0.01% overhead. Weekly checkpoint diffing: compare `torch.max(abs(ckpt_new - ckpt_old))` per layer — a corrupted weight shows as a single outlier of 100× the normal per-step delta.

Normal per-sample cross-entropy loss ≈ 1.8 nats (averaged over tokens). A garbage-target sample where the model assigns ~0.001 probability to each target token: per-token loss = $-\ln(0.001) = 6.9$ nats. With standard PyTorch `CrossEntropyLoss(reduction='mean')`, loss is averaged over all tokens across the batch. Batch: 32 samples × 512 tokens = 16,384 tokens total. Good tokens: 31 × 512 = 15,872 tokens at 1.8 nats each. Bad tokens: 512 tokens at 6.9 nats each. Batch mean = $(15,872 \times 1.8 + 512 \times 6.9) / 16,384 = (28,570 + 3,533) / 16,384 ≈ $ **1.96 nats** — barely noticeable. To produce a spike to 45.0, the loss must use `reduction='sum'` or the sample is far more pathological: e.g., a truncated sequence decoded as out-of-vocabulary garbage where the model assigns ~$e^{-45}$ probability per token, producing per-token loss of ~45 nats. With per-sample sum reported as batch loss: one sample with 512 × 45 = 23,040 nats dominates the batch sum. The gradient norm from this sample is ~$45/1.8 = 25\times$ normal per-token, and concentrated in the embedding/output layers for the garbage tokens. Without per-sample gradient clipping, this single sample's gradient dominates the entire update, pushing all 7B parameters in a nonsensical direction. Recovery takes ~200 steps because the learning rate is small enough that 200 normal updates gradually undo the damage.

A mixed-precision training checkpoint stores: **FP16 weights** = 70B × 2 bytes = 140 GB. **FP32 master weights** (maintained by the optimizer for numerical stability) = 70B × 4 bytes = 280 GB. **Total: 420 GB** = 6 bytes per parameter. The 'extra' 280 GB is the FP32 master copy of the weights. **Where are Adam's m and v states?** In a full optimizer checkpoint, Adam adds: m (first moment) = 70B × 4 bytes = 280 GB, v (second moment) = 70B × 4 bytes = 280 GB. A *full* training checkpoint would be 140 + 280 + 280 + 280 = **980 GB** (14 bytes/param). The 420 GB file contains only weights (FP16 + FP32 master) without optimizer states — likely a 'weights-only' checkpoint. **Can you delete the FP32 master weights?** Yes, for deployment. Inference only needs the FP16 weights (140 GB). The FP32 copy exists solely to maintain training precision during optimizer updates. Deleting it saves 280 GB (67% reduction) with zero impact on inference quality.

**Distillation cost:** 32× A100 × $2.20/hr × 24 hr × 14 days = **$23,654** one-time. | **Current serving (70B on 8× H100):** $180,000/month (as given — includes multi-region redundancy, load balancing, and operational overhead). | **Student serving (7B on A10G):** A 7B model is 10× smaller but A10G is ~5× slower than H100 for inference. To match the 70B system's throughput: ~4 A10Gs per region. With the same multi-region setup (assume 3 regions): 12 × $0.75/hr × 730 hr/month = **$6,570/month** for compute. Add operational overhead proportional to original: ~$10,000/month. **Student total ≈ $16,570/month**. | **Monthly savings:** $180,000 − $16,570 = **$163,430/month**. | **Payback period:** $23,654 / $163,430 = **0.14 months (~4 days)**. | **Annual analysis:** If the teacher is updated quarterly, re-distill 4×/year = $94,616/year. Annual serving savings = $163,430 × 12 = $1.96M. **Net savings: ~$1.87M/year** — if the quality gap is acceptable. A 5% drop in task accuracy on a revenue-critical endpoint could easily cost more than $1.87M in lost conversions or customer churn. The decision hinges on measuring the quality–cost trade-off, not just the compute math.

**30 FPS target = 33.3ms per-frame deadline.** Current pipeline: model inference + post-processing (NMS). On open roads (~10 objects): inference = 25ms, NMS = 2ms → total 27ms → 30 FPS. **Dense intersection (~50 objects):** NMS scales super-linearly with detections. NMS time jumps from 2ms to 8ms. Additional model overhead from denser feature maps: inference rises to 28ms. Total: 28 + 8 = **36ms → 27.8 FPS**, which rounds to ~22 FPS with OS jitter and frame drops. **Adaptive quality ladder:** | **Rung 0 (Full quality):** 28ms inference + 8ms NMS = 36ms — misses 33.3ms deadline. | **Rung 1 (Reduced resolution, 640→480):** ~18ms inference + 5ms NMS (fewer detections at lower res) = 23ms — **10ms headroom**. | **Rung 2 (Smallest model + aggressive NMS threshold):** ~12ms inference + 2ms NMS = 14ms — **19ms headroom** for worst-case scenes. | **Trigger:** monitor per-frame latency with an exponential moving average. When EMA > 30ms, step down one rung. When EMA < 25ms for 10 consecutive frames, step up. | **Result over 10 seconds in dense intersection:** Rung 0 (drop frames): delivers ~220 frames. Rung 1 (steady): delivers **300 frames** at slightly reduced detection range — no dropped frames, no safety gaps.

Vibration: 100 Hz, 0.5mm peak amplitude. Exposure: 10ms. During a 10ms exposure at 100 Hz, the camera completes exactly one full vibration cycle. The sensor integrates light over the full range of motion: peak-to-peak displacement = 2 × 0.5mm = **1.0mm of motion blur**. Pixel pitch: 10mm FOV / 2592 pixels = 3.9μm. Blur in pixels: 1000μm / 3.9μm = **256 pixels** — the image is smeared across 256 pixels during each exposure. At 1ms exposure (1/10th of vibration period): displacement ≈ 0.5mm × sin(2π × 100 × 0.001) ≈ 0.5mm × sin(0.628) ≈ 0.5 × 0.588 = 0.294mm = **75 pixels** of blur — still too much. At 0.1ms exposure: displacement ≈ 0.5mm × 2π × 100 × 0.0001 ≈ 0.031mm = **8 pixels** — borderline acceptable. At 0.05ms exposure: ~4 pixels — acceptable for defect detection. Light requirement at 0.05ms vs 10ms: **200× more illumination** needed. LED ring solution: high-intensity strobed LED at 200× continuous power for 0.05ms pulses. Alternative: vibration-damping mount with 100× attenuation at 10:1 frequency ratio allows 10ms exposure. Accelerometer trigger (ADXL345, $3, 3200 Hz sample rate): capture frames only at zero-crossing of vibration cycle, when instantaneous velocity is at maximum but displacement is near zero — reducing effective blur to <5 pixels.

A camera module might draw 50mW in sleep mode and 2W during active inference. If inference takes 100ms and runs every 5 seconds, the duty cycle is 2% active, 98% sleep. Average power = $(2W \times 0.1s + 0.05W \times 4.9s) / 5s = (0.200 + 0.245) / 5 = 0.445 / 5 = $ **0.089W**. Compare to continuous 2W operation: a **22.5× reduction** in average power. With a 10Wh battery: continuous = 10 / 2 = 5 hours. Duty-cycled = 10 / 0.089 = **112 hours (~4.7 days)**. Adding a PIR pre-trigger (0.1mW) that wakes the camera only on motion: if motion occurs 10% of the time, effective duty cycle drops to 0.2%. Average power ≈ 0.05W (sleep dominates) → **200 hours (~8.3 days)** on 10Wh.

Drone: 250W flight + 4.6W AI = 254.6W → 99.9/254.6 = 23.5 min. AI's share: 4.6/254.6 = 1.8% of power. Ground robot: 20W base + 4.6W AI = 24.6W → 99.9/24.6 = 4.1 hours. AI's share: 18.7%. Energy per inference: 9W × 15ms = 0.135J. At 30 FPS for 4.1 hours: 30 × 3600 × 4.1 = 442,800 inferences. Total energy = 442,800 × 0.135J = 59,778 J. Converting to Wh: 59,778 / 3,600 = **16.6 Wh** consumed by AI (~17% of battery). The remaining 83.3 Wh powers the base platform (motors, sensors, comms). Note: the 4.6W average AI power already accounts for the duty cycle (9W × 15ms × 30 FPS = 4.05W compute + idle overhead), so these figures are consistent.

Orin at MAXN (60W): 275 TOPS, T_junction hits 80°C in ~120s at 40°C ambient. P-state 1 (~40W): ~180 TOPS → 20 FPS. P-state 2 (~25W): ~110 TOPS → 14 FPS. Voluntary 30W mode from boot: ~150 TOPS sustained indefinitely → steady 18 FPS with no degradation. Total frames over 10 minutes: Throttled mode (MAXN start): 25 × 180s + 20 × 180s + 14 × 240s = 4,500 + 3,600 + 3,360 = **11,460 frames**. Steady 30W mode: 18 × 600s = **10,800 frames**. The throttled path delivers ~6% more total frames, but with **unpredictable step transitions** that break downstream object tracking (track IDs are lost when frames are missed during thermal transitions). For safety-critical perception, the steady 18 FPS mode is superior: consistent inter-frame intervals maintain tracking continuity, and the system never enters a degraded state that operators must monitor.

Solar: 80Wh/day. Fixed overhead: 12.5Wh/day (always-on system components). Remaining energy budget: 80 − 12.5 = 67.5Wh. Active power: 7.5W. Idle power: 1W. **Max active time** (must account for idle power during non-active hours): 7.5W × t_active + 1W × (24 − t_active) = 67.5Wh → 6.5 × t_active + 24 = 67.5 → t_active = **6.7 hours/day**. (Note: simply dividing 67.5Wh / 7.5W = 9 hours is wrong because it ignores the 1W idle draw during the remaining 17.3 hours, which consumes 17.3Wh.) At 10 inf/s: 6.7 × 3600 × 10 = **241K inferences/day**. With 20% battery reserve for cloudy days: effective active time = 6.7 × 0.8 = 5.4 hours → **194K inferences/day**. Battery buffer: can sustain ~13 hours of active operation without sun (100Wh / 7.5W), providing ~1.25 cloudy days of buffer (100Wh / 80Wh daily consumption).
